title: An 'It Chapter Two' Deep Dive, 'Joker' at Venice, and Big Winners at the Toronto International Film Festival | The Oscars Show
format: The Oscars-show edition: Joker takes Venice's Golden Lion, Toronto sorts the contenders from the also-rans, and the Best Supporting Actress field fills up. Then director Andy Muschietti and, separately, Jason Concepcion on It Chapter Two.

segments.heading: Joker Wins the Golden Lion
segments.note: Venice and the early Oscar noise

films.title: Joker
films.note: Golden Lion winner; Sean's read is a Taxi Driver riff by way of Todd Phillips. Neither host has seen it, but they note the Academy already rewarded the character once, via Heath Ledger.

films.title: Taxi Driver
films.note: The 70s touchstone the hosts keep comparing Joker to.

films.title: An Officer and a Spy
films.note: Roman Polanski's Silver Lion winner, awkward for an Academy that expelled him.

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: One of only two recent Golden Lion winners to become a Best Picture nominee.

films.title: Roma
films.note: The other; the pair cite both as evidence Venice may be turning into a bellwether.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: The reactionary-vote precedent they keep circling back to.

films.title: Jojo Rabbit
films.note: Taika Waititi's anti-hate satire; a mixed, faintly disappointed reception, though Scott Feinberg noted audiences liked it more than critics.

films.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
films.note: Tom Hanks confirmed as a supporting player; a warm-hug movie with audience-award appeal.

films.title: Knives Out
films.note: Rian Johnson's Agatha Christie romp, loved by everyone because no awards weight is on it.

films.title: Dolemite Is My Name
films.note: Eddie Murphy in caps; a Netflix quality play, and Wesley Snipes is back.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: The festival's most popular title, carrying the weight of being the biggest darling in Toronto.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: Big rapture for Adam Driver and Laura Dern; Amanda refuses to read the tweets until she sees it.

films.title: Waves
films.note: Warmly received in the same TIFF wave.

films.title: Hustlers
films.note: Lorene Scafaria's adaptation of Jessica Pressler's stripper-heist piece; the story is J-Lo.

films.title: The Two Popes
films.note: The unflashy Netflix crowd-pleaser Sean thinks gets the streamer over the hurdle. Also: pizza.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: Cited in the Netflix-vs-theaters standoff; the Scotiabank Cineplex wouldn't screen streaming films.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: The two-month obsession now resting quietly on the sidelines.

segments.heading: The Best Supporting Actress Field
segments.note: a lot of real people in the mix

films.title: Hustlers
films.note: J-Lo's vehicle; Sean argues supporting is her lane, with Constance Wu the actual lead.

films.title: The Report
films.note: Annette Bening as Dianne Feinstein; a transformation, if not her best, from an actress who's never won.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: Laura Dern, clearly modeled on a real person, whether or not she's a hero.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Margot Robbie, another real person, with real Academy affection behind the movie.

films.title: The Farewell
films.note: Zhao Shuzhen as Lulu Wang's grandmother, the kind of older-actress role the Academy tends to surface late.

films.title: Ford v Ferrari
films.note: Caitriona Balfe stuck with the underwritten traditional-wife role; if Claire Foy didn't get in, no one should for it.

films.title: Cats
films.note: Jennifer Hudson singing Memory, and Judi Dench, why not.

films.title: The Goldfinch
films.note: Nicole Kidman in a film that got dunked on harder than anything in a while.

films.title: Downton Abbey
films.note: Maggie Smith, who won't even show for the Emmys, so an Oscar campaign seems unlikely.

films.title: The Laundromat
films.note: Meryl Streep in a bucket hat. Respect the bucket hat.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: The big unknown; Saoirse Ronan the presumed lead, everyone else fair game.

films.title: Dark Waters
films.note: Anne Hathaway on the long-shot list.

films.title: Bombshell
films.note: Margot Robbie possibly canceling herself out here while she cancels others.

films.title: Ad Astra
films.note: Ruth Negga, loved by Sean but without the screen time to warrant it.

films.title: Avengers: Endgame
films.note: Scarlett Johansson potentially running against herself, this and Jojo Rabbit against her Marriage Story lead.

segments.heading: Andy Muschietti on It Chapter Two

films.title: It Chapter Two
films.note: The 2h49m conclusion; Muschietti on the clubhouse he finally got to build, Paul Bunyan (King's request), the upside-down blood set, and casting Peter Bogdanovich as the on-set director.

films.title: It
films.note: The scrappier, budget-limited first chapter that green-lit the sequel.

films.title: Mama
films.note: Muschietti's feature debut, roughly a decade before this.

films.title: Paper Moon
films.note: His favorite Bogdanovich, the reason he pursued casting Peter.

films.title: Close Encounters of the Third Kind
films.note: The Spielberg-reshot-after-release model for his dreamed six-hour supercut.

films.title: La La Land
films.note: The reason a new musical feels hard; Chazelle did it first.

films.title: Midsommar
films.note: The recent film that wowed him; he loves Ari Aster's savage streak of humor.

films.title: Hereditary
films.note: Aster's prior, which Muschietti calls phenomenal.

films.title: Jaws
films.note: The childhood-imprint movie Sean floats first.

films.title: The Shining
films.note: Among the films that imprinted on him as a kid.

films.title: Star Wars
films.note: Ditto.

films.title: The Black Stallion
films.note: Left a mark; he notes Caleb Deschanel shot it.

films.title: The NeverEnding Story
films.note: Meant so much to him, non-horror but pure goosebumps.

segments.heading: Jason Concepcion on It Chapter Two

films.title: It Chapter Two
films.note: Concepcion found it a slog: a three-hour therapy metaphor with a CGI crab clown, saved mainly by Bill Hader; the sequential character arcs deliver the same scene six times.

films.title: It
films.note: He liked chapter one more; the jump scares and Stand-by-Me coming-of-age vibe worked.

films.title: Stand by Me
films.note: His King non-horror favorite; gets the one-special-summer feeling right.

films.title: The Shawshank Redemption
films.note: The other King adaptation he ranks at the very top.

films.title: The Shining
films.note: Unfaithful to the book but nails the vibe and the metaphor of madness.

films.title: Carrie
films.note: The King horror adaptation he thinks really works, off-charts Sissy Spacek and a shock ending.

films.title: The Mist
films.note: Very good, unfaithful, with a gut-punch twist ending.

films.title: Cujo
films.note: Invoked via the addiction context; King has said he doesn't remember writing it.

films.title: Split
films.note: James McAvoy's star wattage, part of the point about a cast credibility gap.

films.title: The Last Jedi
films.note: Concepcion's parting note: just rewatched it, Adam Driver kicks ass, both high on the hosts' list.

A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood

Close Encounters of the Third Kind.title: Close Encounters of the Third Kind
